selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":33,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"waiter/waitress","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"master's degree","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["photography","travel","cycling","videogames"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":32,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":23,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":18,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":51,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":48,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

persona = {"age":33,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"waiter/waitress","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"master's degree","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["photography","travel","cycling","videogames"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":32,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":23,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":18,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":51,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":48,"level":"moderate"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode":"human",
  "topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion":"As a person who spends just one hour a day on social media, I've seen enough misinformation to form my own thoughts. My conservative nature makes me skeptical of things that seem too perfect or dramatic. When I first encountered viral hoaxes about celebrities during my time working as a waiter in New York city, I felt genuinely concerned because our customers could easily get caught up without discerning the truth. Even with a vocational diploma from an educational institution influenced by my parents' backgrounds—the master's degree holder and lower secondary-educated parent—I've learned enough to be wary of sensational claims. The Hindu beliefs instill caution against blind acceptance; we value evidence-based knowledge over hearsay, much like how I carefully vet food sources while working in hospitality now that I'm self-employed here in America after being native-born all my life. My moderate agreeableness keeps me from outright dismissing anything without due consideration for others' perspectives too.",
  "reasoning_summary":"Firstly, based on limited daily usage (one hour), exposure to fake content occurs but not frequently enough to dominate experience entirely.\nSecondly, the low openness and conscientiousness scores suggest a preference for straightforward information over abstract concepts or deep analysis.\nThirdly, being married with children aligns with traditional family values that may help maintain perspective on trivial online disputes.\nFourthly, as native-born American from Hinduism background, there's likely an appreciation for diverse viewpoints without accepting every claim at face value.\nFifthly, hobbies like photography require attention to detail which could transfer into being more discerning about visual media authenticity.",
  "tone":"Skeptical yet balanced",
  "citations":[],
  "safety_notes":"none",
  "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","sexual_orientation","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature","religious_beliefs","time_onsocialmedia","hobbies","ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a person who spends just one hour a day on social media, I've seen enough misinformation to form my own thoughts. My conservative nature makes me skeptical of things that seem too perfect or dramatic. When I first encountered viral hoaxes about celebrities during my time working as a waiter in New York city, I felt genuinely concerned because our customers could easily get caught up without discerning the truth. Even with a vocational diploma from an educational institution influenced by my parents' backgrounds—the master's degree holder and lower secondary-educated parent—I've learned enough to be wary of sensational claims. The Hindu beliefs instill caution against blind acceptance; we value evidence-based knowledge over hearsay, much like how I carefully vet food sources while working in hospitality now that I'm self-employed here in America after being native-born all my life. My moderate agreeableness keeps me from outright dismissing anything without due consideration for others' perspectives too.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Firstly, based on limited daily usage (one hour), exposure to fake content occurs but not frequently enough to dominate experience entirely.
Secondly, the low openness and conscientiousness scores suggest a preference for straightforward information over abstract concepts or deep analysis.
Thirdly, being married with children aligns with traditional family values that may help maintain perspective on trivial online disputes.
Fourthly, as native-born American from Hinduism background, there's likely an appreciation for diverse viewpoints without accepting every claim at face value.
Fifthly, hobbies like photography require attention to detail which could transfer into being more discerning about visual media authenticity.
